concerns.text: The plan defines the bias metric as (E_down(k) - E_true(k)) / E_true(k). However, E_down(k) is generated by Fourier truncation of E_true(k). By definition, for k > k_Nyquist_down, E_down(k) is exactly 0. The bias at these wavenumbers is therefore -100% by mathematical identity, not an empirical measurement of resolution error. The analysis must explicitly exclude k > k_Nyquist_down from the 'bias curve' or the result is a tautology.

The plan proposes fitting scaling exponents (e.g., -5/3) to the downsampled data and comparing them to the ground truth. However, Fourier truncation (sharp spectral filter) introduces Gibbs phenomena (oscillations) in real space. This artifact will systematically distort the structure function S_p(r) at small scales (r < 2 * grid_spacing_down). Fitting a power law in this region without explicitly masking the 'Gibbs-affected' scales will yield a biased exponent that is a deterministic function of the filter, not a stochastic property of the flow. The plan must define a 'valid fitting range' that excludes scales affected by the truncation filter.

The plan assumes 1024³ is the 'ground truth' for bias quantification. In turbulence physics, 1024³ simulations (Re_lambda ~ 400-500) still exhibit finite-Reynolds-number effects and numerical dissipation at the highest resolved wavenumbers. Treating the 1024³ spectrum as the 'true' Kolmogorov limit for the purpose of validating the 1024/16 (64³) case is scientifically unsound if the 1024³ data itself is not converged at the scales being compared. The plan must clarify if the 'ground truth' is the 1024³ dataset itself (relative bias) or if a higher-resolution reference (e.g., 2048³) is required to validate the inertial subrange scaling of the lower resolutions.
